entries.summary: P1 opened by taking the northeast center and staging the guardian/marksman core toward the middle.
entries.reasoning: P1 followed the compact-center assignment: the raider took the nearest supply center while the scout and slow core advanced without overextending. The response-window unit-count rule was not relevant before the first income cycle.

entries.summary: P2 started the west-south flank and sent the second scout toward the southeast lane.
entries.reasoning: P2 used scouts for lane access while the druid and marksman stayed behind them. The flank player prioritized early captures over a central collision.

entries.summary: P1 advanced the center group but banked supply instead of recruiting immediately.
entries.reasoning: P1 had only 5 saved supply after income, so recruit cost 6 kept the central player on starting bodies for another turn.

entries.summary: P2 widened the southern approach and also stayed below the first recruit threshold.
entries.reasoning: P2 had 5 supply after income. The scouts continued to stretch P1 toward center-south and southeast while the support pair trailed.

entries.summary: P1 claimed center-north and recruited the first central marksman reinforcement.
entries.reasoning: The delayed recruit was a marksman rather than another scout, matching P1s plan to protect a compact central formation.

entries.summary: P2 completed the southeast flank, chipped P1s raider, and recruited a punish raider.
entries.reasoning: P2 reached 8 supply and spent 6 on one raider, banking the rest because the second recruit threshold was still out of reach.

entries.summary: P1 took the exact middle, bloodied the forward southern scout, and added a guardian.
entries.reasoning: P1 converted the delayed central income into durability rather than a faster flank unit. The new guardian entered inactive at home.

entries.summary: P2 took center-south and recruited support behind the flank.
entries.reasoning: P2 chose preservation and lane pressure over a direct middle trade. The support recruit kept the flank plan alive without matching P1s center mass.

entries.summary: P1 collapsed onto the middle and killed the damaged southern scout.
entries.reasoning: The center raider finished the scout through a legal melee attack. P2 still had an active southeast scout and a support pocket, so the flank plan remained relevant.

entries.summary: P2 flipped east and damaged P1s exposed center scout from a support marksman position.
entries.reasoning: The surviving flank scout forced P1 to respect the east lane. P2 avoided a losing central melee and used ranged pressure to soften the middle holder.

entries.summary: P1 killed P2s support marksman but had to spread east instead of staying compact.
entries.reasoning: The center formation punished the exposed marksman, while the guardian and marksman shifted toward the east scout. This preserved the center/flank split.

entries.summary: P2 killed P1s damaged raider and reloaded the west lane with a scout.
entries.reasoning: P2 converted the delayed raider into a real punish turn. The cost-6 economy allowed only one recruit, so the response restored pressure without flooding the board.

entries.summary: P1 damaged the east scout, reclaimed the lane, and recruited a third marksman.
entries.reasoning: P1 could not legally bring the guardian into the east scout this turn, so the ranged units left it badly damaged instead of forcing an illegal finish. P1 spent 6 supply on one marksman and banked the remainder.

entries.summary: P2 re-flipped west-south and sent the raider back into the center-south lane.
entries.reasoning: P2 could not match P1s center mass directly, so the new scout took capture duty while the raider threatened P1s lead guardian.

entries.summary: P1 retook east and damaged the raider, but chose another guardian over a second recruit wave.
entries.reasoning: P1 had a lead but not a resolved win. The center guardian hit the raider without enough follow-up to kill it, leaving P2 a live punish piece.

entries.summary: P2 used the surviving raider to kill P1s east scout and recruited one more support unit.
entries.reasoning: P2 reduced P1s unit lead and kept the flank alive. Under the lead-4 ruleset, any pending P1 threat would clear only if the lead fell below four before P1s next start check.

entries.summary: P1 split the center attacks, killing both the forward scout and the damaged flank raider.
entries.reasoning: P1s compact center finally produced a two-kill turn and added one body. This created a large board lead, but no pending unit-count threat existed yet because P1 had not started a turn with that lead.

entries.summary: P2 recruited once and pulled the remaining flank units inward, but could not remove a P1 unit.
entries.reasoning: P2 spent 6 of 9 available supply on one marksman and had only 3 supply left, so a second recruit was not legal. P1 would start the next turn at 8 units to 5, only a three-unit lead, so the lead-4 response window did not create a pending threat.

entries.summary: P1 widened the central lead, killed a support healer, and recruited another marksman.
entries.reasoning: Start-of-turn response-window check: P1 had 8 living units to P2s 5, a three-unit lead, so no lead-4 pending threat was recorded. P1 then used the central guardian line and deck tempo to upgrade P1-guardian-2, remove P2-healer-1, wound P2-druid-1, spend 6 of 8 supply on P1-marksman-4, and leave P2 a chance to pull the count back below the lead-4 threshold before P1s next start check.

entries.summary: P2 killed one central guardian and recruited a scout, reducing P1 below the lead-4 threshold before any pending threat existed.
entries.reasoning: P2s response prevented the previous P1 board-phase lead from becoming a pending lead-4 threat. P2 healed P2-druid-1 before the druid and raider combined to remove P1-guardian-1, then spent 6 of 8 supply on P2-scout-4. That changed the count from P1 9 vs P2 4 to P1 8 vs P2 5, only a three-unit P1 lead, so P1 would not record a pending threat at the start of round 11.

entries.summary: P1 started below the lead-4 trigger, then used two central marksmen to remove the druid and recruited a raider.
entries.reasoning: Start-of-turn check: P1 led 8 units to 5, so no pending lead-4 threat existed. P1 used P1-marksman-1 and the repositioned P1-marksman-2 to attack P2-druid-1 for lethal damage, assigning one deck damage to each legal marksman attack and respecting the per-attacker damage cap. P1 gained 6 supply from four centers, spent 6 on P1-raider-3, and ended at P1 9 vs P2 4. Because pending threats are recorded only at start-of-turn checks, this was not yet a pending win.

entries.summary: P2 preserved the flank count with a healer recruit, but only wounded the forward P1 marksman.
entries.reasoning: No pending P1 threat existed yet, but P2 needed to reduce the board lead before P1s next start check. P2-raider-2 moved into the vacated center and hit P1-marksman-1 for 7 total damage, assigning Zap damage to that legal raider attack and leaving the marksman at 1 HP rather than removing it. P2 gained 5 supply from three centers, spent 6 on P2-healer-3, and ended at P1 9 vs P2 5. That exact four-unit gap means P1 will record a pending lead-4 threat at the start of turn 23.

entries.summary: P1 recorded the first pending lead-4 threat, removed the flank raider, and recruited a guardian.
entries.reasoning: Start-of-turn response-window check: P1 had 9 living units to P2s 5 and no existing pending threat, so P1 recorded a pending lead-4 unit-count win threat and continued. P1 then used the two central marksmen, with deck damage attached only to their legal attacks and respecting the one-extra-damage-per-attacker cap, to remove P2-raider-2. P1 gained 6 supply, spent 6 on P1-guardian-5, and ended at P1 10 vs P2 4. P2 now gets a full response turn and must reduce the lead below four to clear the pending threat.

entries.summary: P2 used the full response turn to recruit and consolidate, but could not reduce the pending lead-4 threat.
entries.reasoning: P2 was responding to a pending P1 lead-4 threat from turn 23. The flank support pieces were too far from damaged P1-marksman-1 to finish a kill this turn, so P2 preserved material by moving support inward and spending exactly 6 supply on P2-marksman-3. The final count became P1 10 vs P2 5, still a five-unit P1 lead. Because the lead was not reduced below four, the pending P1 threat survives and P1 wins at the beginning of round 13 before any further actions.
